front: At what gestational age does sucking-swallowing coordination mature enough for oral feeding?
back: By 34–36 weeks gestation, sucking-swallowing-breathing coordination matures enough for oral feeding. Before 34 weeks, nasogastric tube feeds are required. Preterm infants also lack sufficient lower esophageal sphincter tone and gastric emptying maturity.

front: What embryologic event, when disrupted, causes intestinal malrotation?
back: Failure of the normal 270-degree counterclockwise rotation of the midgut around the superior mesenteric artery during weeks 6–12 of gestation. This leaves the cecum in the right upper quadrant and creates Ladd bands that can obstruct the duodenum.

front: What distinguishes physiologic GER from pathologic GERD in a 3-month-old infant?
back: Physiologic GER is effortless regurgitation without distress, weight loss, or feeding refusal — present in up to 50% of healthy infants. GERD implies esophageal injury or symptoms causing impairment (arching, persistent crying, feeding refusal, poor weight gain). Treatment for GER is reassurance; GERD requires evaluation.

front: What is the approximate small intestinal length at term birth and why does it matter?
back: Approximately 200–250 cm at term. This baseline determines short bowel syndrome risk after surgical resection — loss of more than 50% significantly impairs absorption. Preterm bowel grows 3–4× in the first year, so neonatal resection outcomes improve with time.

front: Which intestinal enzyme matures last in premature infants, explaining transient lactose intolerance in the NICU?
back: Lactase is the last brush-border enzyme to mature, reaching adult activity only near term (36–40 weeks). However, colonic bacterial fermentation of unabsorbed lactose usually compensates, and persistent clinical lactose intolerance is uncommon.

front: What is the primary risk factor for necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) in premature infants?
back: Prematurity is the dominant risk factor — NEC occurs in ~7–10% of infants <1500 g. Additional factors: formula feeding (versus breast milk), bacterial colonization dysbiosis, intestinal hypoperfusion, and immature mucosal immunity. Donor breast milk reduces NEC risk when maternal milk is unavailable.

front: What is Bell staging for NEC and when does management change?
back: Stage I: suspected (apnea, feed intolerance); Stage II: confirmed (pneumatosis intestinalis on X-ray); Stage III: advanced (perforation, peritonitis, shock). Stage IIB/III requires NPO, IV antibiotics, and surgical consult; Stage III with perforation requires emergent laparotomy or peritoneal drain.

front: What hormone is elevated in Prader-Willi syndrome, causing hyperphagia?
back: Ghrelin levels are markedly elevated in Prader-Willi syndrome, causing insatiable hunger and hyperphagia. Leptin deficiency causes a separate monogenic obesity syndrome. GLP-1 and PYY promote satiety post-meal and are reduced in obesity.

front: What is the Migrating Motor Complex (MMC) and what is its clinical relevance?
back: The MMC is an interdigestive cyclical motor pattern (approximately every 90 minutes in the fasted state) that clears residual food and bacteria from the small intestine. It is mediated by motilin. Disruption of the MMC predisposes to SIBO. It is absent in the postprandial state.

front: What is meconium and when should failure to pass it prompt evaluation?
back: Meconium is the first stool — composed of amniotic fluid, mucus, bile, and intestinal cells. Term infants should pass meconium within 48 hours of birth. Failure to pass by 48 hours raises concern for Hirschsprung disease, meconium plug, or cystic fibrosis (meconium ileus).

front: What is the significance of bilious vomiting in a neonate?
back: Bilious (green) vomiting in a neonate is a surgical emergency until proven otherwise. The most critical cause is malrotation with midgut volvulus — which can cause complete mesenteric ischemia within hours. Upper GI series is the diagnostic test of choice.

front: What are the caloric requirements for a term neonate versus a 1-year-old child?
back: Term neonates require approximately 100–120 kcal/kg/day. By 12 months, requirements decrease to approximately 80–100 kcal/kg/day. Premature infants require 110–135 kcal/kg/day for adequate growth. These benchmarks guide parenteral and enteral nutrition prescribing.

front: What are the normal gastric acid secretion characteristics in neonates?
back: Newborns have transient achlorhydria in the first 24 hours followed by rapid acid secretion onset. By approximately 3 months, gastric acid output reaches adult levels on a weight-adjusted basis. This is why H. pylori transmission is most dangerous in infancy.

front: What is the normal lower esophageal sphincter (LES) pressure and what reduces it?
back: Normal LES resting pressure: 15–35 mmHg. It is reduced by fatty meals, caffeine, chocolate, peppermint, theophylline, calcium channel blockers, anticholinergics, and transient LES relaxations (TLESRs) — the dominant mechanism of GER in children.

front: What formula modification reduces regurgitation in healthy infants with GER?
back: Thickened feeds (rice or oat cereal 1 tablespoon per 2 oz, or pre-thickened anti-reflux formulas) reduce visible regurgitation. Per NASPGHAN 2022 guidelines, thickening is preferred over PPIs for uncomplicated infant GER to address the symptom without pharmacologic risk.

front: What is the definition of normal stool frequency in a term breastfed infant at 2 months?
back: Breastfed infants at 2 months typically stool 3–5 times per day, but after 6 weeks of age may normally stool as infrequently as once per week — breast milk is absorbed so efficiently that little residue remains. Formula-fed infants stool 4–5 times per day and less variably.

front: What GI symptoms are considered alarm features requiring urgent evaluation?
back: Alarm features: involuntary weight loss, rectal bleeding, nocturnal symptoms awakening from sleep, fever, persistent vomiting, dysphagia, family history of IBD/celiac/peptic ulcer, right upper or lower quadrant pain, perianal disease, and growth failure. These should not be attributed to functional disorders without investigation.

front: What does the Rome IV classification system define?
back: Rome IV (2016) defines functional GI disorders (FGIDs) as disorders of gut-brain interaction characterized by chronic GI symptoms without structural or biochemical explanation. It uses symptom-based diagnostic criteria with specified symptom duration thresholds. The pediatric criteria are age-stratified (neonate/toddler vs. child/adolescent).

front: What is the significance of perianal skin tags and fistulas in a child with chronic diarrhea?
back: Perianal skin tags, fissures, fistulas, and abscesses strongly suggest Crohn's disease, particularly if multiple or in unusual locations. A single midline posterior fissure is usually benign and associated with constipation. Perianal Crohn's can precede luminal disease and requires anti-TNF therapy.

front: What findings on abdominal examination suggest peritoneal irritation?
back: Guarding (voluntary or involuntary muscle rigidity), rebound tenderness (Blumberg sign), rigidity, and absent bowel sounds. In children, percussion tenderness is often more reliable than rebound. These findings indicate peritonitis and require surgical evaluation.

front: How does the character of vomiting help localize GI pathology?
back: Bilious vomiting = obstruction distal to the ampulla of Vater (malrotation, duodenal atresia). Non-bilious projectile vomiting in a 2–8 week infant = pyloric stenosis. Hematemesis = upper GI source. Feculent vomiting = distal small bowel or colonic obstruction.

front: What laboratory tests constitute the initial workup for a child with suspected malabsorption?
back: CBC (anemia), CMP (albumin, liver enzymes), ESR/CRP, anti-tTG IgA + total IgA (celiac), stool studies (fecal calprotectin, elastase, fat, culture, O&P, C. diff toxin), TSH. Consider sweat chloride if CF features present. Imaging is secondary to labs in initial workup.

front: What is fecal calprotectin and what threshold warrants endoscopy?
back: Fecal calprotectin (FC) is a neutrophil-derived protein reflecting intestinal inflammation. FC <50 mcg/g makes IBD unlikely; FC >200 mcg/g warrants colonoscopy. FC distinguishes IBD from IBS (sensitivity ~80%, specificity ~85%). Elevated FC is also seen in infectious enteritis, NEC, and polyps.

front: What imaging modality is preferred for evaluating small bowel Crohn's disease?
back: MRI enterography (MRE) is preferred — no radiation, excellent soft tissue contrast, mucosal and transmural assessment, evaluation of fistulas and abscesses. CT enterography is used when MRE is unavailable or in emergencies. Ultrasound is radiation-free but operator-dependent.

front: What is the Rome IV age threshold distinguishing infant/toddler from child/adolescent functional GI disorders?
back: Age 4 years is the threshold. Infants/toddlers (0–4 years) are classified separately (infant colic, functional constipation, cyclic vomiting, functional diarrhea of infancy). Children and adolescents (4–18 years) use criteria parallel to adult Rome IV — including IBS, functional dyspepsia, functional abdominal pain NOS.

front: What is the approach to a child with recurrent abdominal pain and a normal workup?
back: After excluding organic causes proportional to alarm features, diagnose a functional GI disorder by positive Rome IV criteria. Explain the brain-gut axis model to the family. First-line: dietary modifications, reassurance, school/psychosocial support. Second-line: gut-directed hypnotherapy, CBT, low-dose amitriptyline or cyproheptadine.

front: What growth parameters should be routinely plotted at every visit for a child with IBD?
back: Height, weight, BMI (plotted on WHO/CDC growth curves), height velocity (cm/year compared to expected for age/Tanner stage), and pubertal staging (Tanner). Growth failure (height velocity <4 cm/year or crossing ≥2 percentile lines) in IBD indicates disease activity, malnutrition, or steroid toxicity.

front: What are the Rome IV functional dyspepsia criteria in children?
back: Functional dyspepsia (age ≥4 years): one or more of — postprandial fullness, early satiation, epigastric pain, or epigastric burning — present ≥4 days/month for ≥2 months, with no structural cause. Two subtypes: postprandial distress syndrome (meal-related) and epigastric pain syndrome (pain/burning not meal-related).

front: What distinguishes organic from functional GI disease in the clinical approach?
back: Organic disease has an identifiable structural, inflammatory, or biochemical cause (celiac, IBD, H. pylori). Functional disorders meet Rome IV criteria after excluding organic causes — investigations should be proportional to alarm features. Both can coexist (e.g., functional IBS post-gastroenteritis).

front: What history features distinguish IBS from IBD in a school-age child?
back: IBS: pain relieved with defecation, altered stool form, no nocturnal diarrhea, no weight loss, no fever, normal CRP/ESR/calprotectin. IBD: nocturnal diarrhea, rectal bleeding, weight loss, fever, elevated inflammatory markers, perianal disease, growth failure. FC >200 mcg/g warrants endoscopic evaluation.

front: What physical examination finding is characteristic of celiac disease?
back: Children with celiac disease often have abdominal distension, loss of subcutaneous fat, muscle wasting (especially buttocks and thighs), and pallor. Dermatitis herpetiformis (intensely pruritic vesicular rash on elbows, knees, buttocks) is pathognomonic of celiac disease.

front: What is the significance of involuntary weight loss in a teenager with abdominal pain?
back: Involuntary weight loss is an alarm feature that must not be attributed to functional pain. Causes include IBD, celiac disease, hyperthyroidism, malabsorption, and malignancy. Workup: BMI trend, CBC, CMP, inflammatory markers, anti-tTG IgA, stool studies, and imaging. Eating disorder must also be considered.

front: What are the minimum biopsy requirements for celiac disease diagnosis?
back: At least 4–6 biopsy specimens from the duodenal bulb (1 from D1) and distal duodenum (D2/D3). The bulb must be included because patchy disease can be missed if only distal duodenum is sampled. Biopsies should be taken even if mucosa appears normal endoscopically.

front: What is the ESPGHAN 2020 no-biopsy pathway for celiac disease diagnosis?
back: In symptomatic children: anti-tTG IgA >10× ULN + positive EMA on a separate blood sample + positive HLA-DQ2/DQ8 allows diagnosis without endoscopy. All three criteria must be met. Total IgA must be confirmed to exclude IgA deficiency.

front: What is the diagnostic yield of wireless capsule endoscopy in pediatric Crohn's disease?
back: Capsule endoscopy detects small bowel Crohn's in approximately 40–70% of cases where ileocolonoscopy and imaging are negative. It is approved for children ≥2 years or ≥10 kg. The main risk is capsule retention (3–5% in suspected Crohn's) — a patency capsule should be used first.

front: What is pH-MII and what does it detect that pH probe alone cannot?
back: pH-MII (combined pH and multichannel intraluminal impedance) detects both acid (pH <4) and non-acid (pH ≥4) reflux events, as well as direction (proximal vs. distal) and composition (liquid vs. gas vs. mixed). pH probe alone misses non-acid reflux — common in infants and patients on PPIs.

front: What is the 4-hour gastric scintigraphy abnormal threshold for gastroparesis?
back: Standardized protocol: technetium-99m sulfur colloid-labeled meal, imaging at 0, 1, 2, and 4 hours. Abnormal: >10% retention at 4 hours. The 4-hour image is the most clinically relevant single time point. A 2-hour retention >60% also supports delayed gastric emptying.

front: What is the preferred initial imaging for suspected intussusception?
back: Abdominal ultrasound is the initial imaging test — sensitivity >95% (target sign: hyperechoic center surrounded by hypoechoic ring). If confirmed and no perforation: air or hydrostatic enema reduction under fluoroscopy or ultrasound guidance. Success rate ~80–90%. Surgery if enema fails or perforation present.

front: When is liver biopsy indicated in a child with elevated transaminases?
back: Indications: persistent transaminase elevation >2× ULN for >6 months without clear diagnosis, suspected AIH (diagnostic and staging), staging of chronic viral hepatitis, evaluation of Wilson disease, alpha-1-AT deficiency, metabolic liver disease, and neonatal cholestasis workup. Coagulopathy must be corrected first.

front: What fecal markers assess intestinal inflammation and pancreatic function?
back: Fecal calprotectin (FC): neutrophil marker for mucosal inflammation (IBD screening/monitoring). Fecal lactoferrin: similar utility. Alpha-1-AT clearance: measures enteric protein loss (PLE) — 24-hour stool + simultaneous serum A1AT. Fecal elastase: pancreatic exocrine function (EPI if <100 mcg/g).

front: What is the sensitivity of anti-tTG IgA for celiac disease?
back: Anti-tTG IgA has sensitivity of 95–98% and specificity of 95–97% for celiac disease. False negatives occur with IgA deficiency (prevalence ~2% in celiac — 10× higher than general population). Always check total IgA simultaneously. If IgA deficient: use anti-tTG IgG or anti-DGP IgG.

front: What is the difference between a DISIDA scan and abdominal ultrasound in neonatal cholestasis?
back: Ultrasound evaluates anatomic structures (gallbladder, bile duct dilation, liver echogenicity). DISIDA scan assesses biliary excretion — non-excretion of tracer into bowel at 24 hours strongly suggests biliary atresia. DISIDA has ~97% sensitivity but ~76% specificity for biliary atresia.

front: What is EndoFLIP and what does it measure in EoE?
back: EndoFLIP (Functional Lumen Imaging Probe) measures esophageal distensibility — the ratio of cross-sectional area to distending pressure. In EoE, distensibility index (DI) <2.8 mm²/mmHg correlates with fibrostenotic disease and food impaction risk. Performed at endoscopy to guide dilation decisions beyond eosinophil counts.

front: What does high-resolution manometry (HRM) diagnose that pH-MII cannot?
back: HRM assesses esophageal motor function — LES pressure, peristalsis, esophagogastric junction morphology. It diagnoses achalasia, ineffective esophageal motility, and jackhammer esophagus. pH-MII assesses reflux events. They answer different questions and are often complementary in complex GERD or dysphagia workups.

front: What is the standard bowel preparation for pediatric colonoscopy?
back: Split-dose PEG 3350 (1 g/kg/dose, max 70 g, given evening before and morning of procedure) is the preferred bowel prep. Colonoscopy can be performed at any age when clinically indicated. Adequate sedation (propofol or ketamine) is required. Clear fluids the day before, nothing by mouth 4–6 hours prior.

front: What are the indications for ERCP in children?
back: ERCP indications: choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction, primary sclerosing cholangitis (stricture dilation/stenting), biliary trauma leak, pancreatic duct disruption or stricture in chronic pancreatitis, and recurrent acute pancreatitis with pancreatic divisum. MRCP provides diagnosis without intervention risk when appropriate.

front: What imaging findings confirm acute appendicitis on ultrasound?
back: Non-compressible appendix >6 mm in outer diameter, surrounded by hyperechoic fat (periappendiceal inflammation), loss of normal echogenic submucosal layer, and free fluid adjacent to the appendix. Color Doppler shows hyperemia. US sensitivity ~86%, specificity ~94% when appendix is visualized — non-visualization rate 30–50%.

front: What is the role of hydrogen breath testing in pediatric GI evaluation?
back: Hydrogen breath tests detect malabsorption (lactose, fructose, sucrose) or SIBO. A rise of >20 ppm above baseline after substrate ingestion indicates malabsorption/fermentation. SIBO breath test uses glucose or lactulose. Antibiotics, colonoscopy prep, and diet alter results.

front: What are the WHO MUAC thresholds for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) and moderate acute malnutrition (MAM)?
back: SAM: MUAC <115 mm (or WHZ <-3 SD, or bilateral pitting edema). MAM: MUAC 115–125 mm (or WHZ -3 to -2 SD). MUAC is the preferred field measure for children 6–59 months — requires only a tape measure and has good predictive validity for mortality risk.

front: What is refeeding syndrome and which electrolytes are most critical?
back: Refeeding syndrome: dangerous electrolyte shifts when nutrition is restarted after prolonged starvation. Carbohydrate reintroduction stimulates insulin, driving phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells. Give thiamine before refeeding (prevents Wernicke encephalopathy). Start feeds at 20 kcal/kg/day and advance slowly.

front: What is the Schofield equation used for in pediatric nutrition?
back: The Schofield equation estimates basal metabolic rate (BMR) in children using weight, height, and age. It is used to calculate total energy expenditure (TEE = BMR × activity factor × stress factor) for enteral and parenteral nutrition prescribing.

front: What distinguishes stunting from wasting in pediatric malnutrition?
back: Wasting: low weight-for-height (WHZ <-2 SD) — reflects acute malnutrition with high short-term mortality risk. Stunting: low height-for-age (HAZ <-2 SD) — reflects chronic malnutrition over months to years, largely irreversible after age 2 (the first 1000 days window).

front: How is BMI percentile interpreted in children compared to adults?
back: In children (2–18 years): Underweight <5th percentile. Normal 5th–84th. Overweight 85th–94th. Obese ≥95th. Severe obesity ≥120% of 95th percentile or ≥35 kg/m² (whichever is lower). Adult BMI cutoffs (25/30) do not apply to children.

front: What is prealbumin and why is it used as a nutritional marker?
back: Prealbumin (transthyretin) has a short half-life of ~2 days (vs. albumin 21 days), making it a more sensitive marker of acute nutritional status changes. Levels fall rapidly with acute illness/inflammation (negative acute-phase reactant) — interpret cautiously when CRP is elevated. Normal range: 15–40 mg/dL.

front: What protein requirements (g/kg/day) apply to term neonates and critically ill children?
back: Term neonates: 2–3 g/kg/day. Infants 1–6 months: 1.5–2.2 g/kg/day. Toddlers 1–3 years: 1.1 g/kg/day. Children 4–13 years: 0.95 g/kg/day. Critically ill children (PICU): 1.5–3 g/kg/day (ASPEN guidelines). Requirements increase with burns, sepsis, and inflammatory bowel disease.

front: What micronutrients are most commonly deficient at celiac disease diagnosis?
back: Iron (most common — duodenal malabsorption), folate, zinc, and vitamin D. Vitamin B12 can be deficient in extensive small bowel disease. Calcium deficiency contributes to osteopenia. Check iron studies, 25-OH vitamin D, folate, B12, and zinc at diagnosis.

front: What is mid-parental height (MPH) and how is it calculated?
back: MPH estimates genetic height potential. Boys: (father's height + mother's height + 13 cm) / 2. Girls: (father's height + mother's height − 13 cm) / 2. Target range is MPH ± 8.5 cm (girls) or ± 10 cm (boys). Height significantly below MPH suggests pathologic growth impairment.

front: What is the ASPEN/ESPGHAN definition of pediatric malnutrition?
back: ASPEN 2015 consensus: ≥1 of: z-score <-2 SD for weight-for-height, BMI-for-age, or length-for-age; MUAC <-2 SD; weight loss ≥5% from usual weight; inadequate dietary intake vs. estimated needs. Severity: mild (<-1 to -2 SD), moderate (-2 to -3 SD), severe (<-3 SD or >10% weight loss).

front: What is the recommended vitamin D supplementation for exclusively breastfed infants?
back: All breastfed infants should receive 400 IU/day of vitamin D starting within the first days of life (AAP recommendation). Breast milk contains only ~20 IU/L of vitamin D — insufficient for adequate levels. Formula-fed infants receiving ≥32 oz/day of vitamin D-fortified formula do not require additional supplementation.

front: What serum markers distinguish kwashiorkor from marasmus?
back: Kwashiorkor: protein-predominant deficiency — low serum albumin (<2.1 g/dL), pitting edema, fatty liver, normal or slightly low weight. Marasmus: severe calorie + protein deficiency — severe wasting with no edema, preserved albumin initially. Edema is the key clinical distinction.

front: What is catch-up growth and at what caloric intake is it typically achieved?
back: Catch-up growth is accelerated linear and weight gain after undernutrition, occurring when nutritional deficits are corrected. It requires energy intakes 150–200% of the RDA for the child's actual age. Rate of catch-up is monitored with serial measurements every 2–4 weeks.

front: What is the Holliday-Segar formula for maintenance fluid calculation?
back: Holliday-Segar: 100 mL/kg/day for first 10 kg + 50 mL/kg/day for 10–20 kg + 20 mL/kg/day for each kg above 20 kg. For enteral nutrition, fluid requirements from formula must be considered in total fluid intake to avoid over- or under-hydration.

front: What is the Pediatric SGNA and when is it used?
back: The Subjective Global Nutritional Assessment (SGNA) is a validated bedside nutritional assessment for children ages 1 month to 18 years. It assesses weight and height history, dietary intake, GI symptoms, functional capacity, and physical signs of malnutrition — rating patients as well-nourished, moderately, or severely malnourished.

front: What is the significance of a low serum albumin in a child with IBD?
back: Hypoalbuminemia in IBD reflects both disease activity (increased protein catabolism from inflammation) and nutritional depletion (inadequate intake, malabsorption). It predicts outcomes (surgical risk, infection susceptibility, poor growth) and guides the need for nutritional support. Albumin normalizes with disease remission and nutritional rehabilitation.

front: What is the NASPGHAN 2022 recommendation on PPIs in healthy infants with suspected GERD?
back: PPIs should NOT be prescribed for healthy infants with uncomplicated regurgitation and crying (infant GER). Clinical trials show no benefit over placebo for crying or regurgitation in this population. Risks include increased respiratory and GI infections, microbiome disruption, and potential bone effects. Most infant GER resolves by 12–18 months.

front: What are the indications for antireflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication) in children?
back: Nissen fundoplication is indicated for: failed maximal medical therapy with confirmed GERD (pH-MII proven), GERD with life-threatening apnea, recurrent aspiration pneumonia from reflux, and neurologically impaired children with severe GERD. Fundoplication failure rate is ~20% in neurologically impaired children.

front: What is the mechanism of transient lower esophageal sphincter relaxations (TLESRs)?
back: TLESRs are vagally mediated, meal-triggered relaxations of the LES lasting 10–45 seconds — longer than normal swallow-induced LES opening (~8 seconds). They are the dominant mechanism of GERD in all age groups. GABA-B agonists (baclofen) reduce TLESR frequency but are limited by CNS side effects.

front: What esophageal complications arise from untreated chronic GERD?
back: Esophagitis (erosive or non-erosive), peptic stricture (causing dysphagia), Barrett's esophagus (intestinal metaplasia — rare in children), and esophageal adenocarcinoma (very rare in pediatrics). Barrett's screening is recommended in adults with chronic GERD >5 years but no pediatric consensus protocol exists.

front: What is Sandifer syndrome?
back: Sandifer syndrome is a movement disorder in infants with GERD characterized by abnormal head and neck posturing (retrocollis, torticollis) during or after feeding. It is a reflex response to esophageal acid exposure and can be mistaken for seizures. Treatment is GERD management; EEG is normal during episodes.

front: What pH-MII parameters define abnormal esophageal acid exposure?
back: Acid exposure time (AET) >6% of total monitoring time at pH <4 is abnormal. In pediatrics, AET >7% is pathologic by most norms. Symptom index (SI) >50% or symptom association probability (SAP) >95% indicates positive symptom-reflux correlation. Total reflux episodes >73/day is also considered elevated.

front: What is the recommended PPI dosing for GERD in children?
back: Omeprazole: 1 mg/kg/day (max 20 mg/day for <20 kg; 20–40 mg/day for ≥20 kg) given 30 minutes before breakfast. Lansoprazole: 1–2 mg/kg/day. Pantoprazole: 0.5–1 mg/kg/day. PPIs given 30 minutes before the first meal achieve maximum efficacy. Initial trial duration: 8–12 weeks.

front: What dietary modifications are evidence-based for GERD management in children?
back: Thickened feeds (infants), smaller more frequent meals, avoidance of caffeine, chocolate, fatty foods, citrus, carbonated beverages, and peppermint. Elevation of head of bed 30 degrees. Weight loss in obese adolescents reduces GERD symptoms significantly.

front: At what age does physiologic infant regurgitation typically resolve?
back: Physiologic infant regurgitation peaks at 4 months (up to 50% of infants daily) and resolves spontaneously in most infants by 12–14 months, coinciding with assumption of upright posture, dietary transition to solids, and LES maturation. Persistence beyond 18 months warrants evaluation.

front: What is the difference between H2RAs and PPIs in GERD treatment?
back: H2RAs (famotidine): block histamine-2 receptors, reduce acid 60–70%, onset 30 minutes, tachyphylaxis develops within weeks. PPIs (omeprazole, lansoprazole): irreversibly block H+/K+ ATPase, reduce acid 90–95%, onset delayed 3–5 days. PPIs are superior for healing esophagitis; both are equivalent for symptom control in non-erosive GERD.

front: What is alginate therapy and when is it used in infant GER?
back: Alginates (e.g., Gaviscon Infant) form a viscous gel raft on gastric contents acting as a physical barrier against reflux. Effective for reducing regurgitation and safe in infants. NASPGHAN 2022 recommends alginates as an alternative to PPIs for uncomplicated infant GER. They do not suppress acid production.

front: What eosinophil count on esophageal biopsy is relevant in a child being treated for GERD?
back: GERD can cause esophageal eosinophilia (typically <15 eos/hpf). EoE is diagnosed when ≥15 eos/hpf persist despite a 2-month PPI trial (or PPI-responsive eosinophilia is now considered EoE subtype). A biopsied child with ≥15 eos/hpf should receive a 2-month PPI trial before diagnosing EoE.

front: What is the relationship between GERD and laryngomalacia?
back: Laryngomalacia is associated with GERD in 65–80% of cases — the relationship is bidirectional (increased inspiratory effort promotes reflux; acid worsens laryngomalacia). Anti-reflux therapy is often prescribed alongside supraglottoplasty. Evidence that GERD treatment improves laryngomalacia outcomes is limited.

front: When is pH-MII indicated over standard pH probe monitoring for GERD evaluation?
back: pH-MII is indicated when: symptoms persist despite PPI therapy (on-therapy testing to assess non-acid reflux), distinguishing non-acid from acid reflux is needed, evaluating atypical symptoms (cough, hoarseness, apnea), and in infants where non-acid reflux predominates. pH-MII is the gold standard for symptom-reflux correlation.

front: What is the management approach when GERD symptoms persist despite twice-daily PPI therapy?
back: Confirm adherence (30 min before meals, correct dose). If compliant, perform pH-MII on therapy to assess acid control and residual reflux. If acid is controlled but symptoms persist (non-acid reflux), consider alginate or baclofen. Rule out EoE. Consider antireflux surgery if confirmed GERD fails maximal medical therapy.

front: What is the relationship between obesity and GERD in adolescents?
back: Obesity increases intra-abdominal pressure, promotes TLESRs, and displaces the esophagogastric junction — all contributing to GERD. BMI is positively correlated with GERD severity and erosive esophagitis in adolescents. Weight loss improves GERD symptoms independent of PPI therapy. All obese adolescents with heartburn should have weight management addressed.

front: What is the diagnostic eosinophil threshold for EoE on esophageal biopsy?
back: At least 15 eosinophils per high-power field (eos/hpf) on esophageal biopsy from at least one location. This threshold persists after a 2-month PPI trial. The peak eosinophil count (not average) is used. Eosinophils are normally absent from esophageal epithelium.

front: What is the 2-food elimination diet (2-FED) and what does it eliminate?
back: The 2-FED removes milk and wheat — the two most common EoE triggers (milk in ~60%, wheat in ~30%). Studies show histologic remission in ~40–50% of patients. It is the least restrictive elimination option and is preferred as first-line dietary therapy in patients willing to trial diet before pharmacotherapy.

front: What FDA-approved pharmacologic therapy is available for EoE in patients ≥12 years?
back: Dupilumab (Dupixent/Eohilia) — an IL-4/IL-13 receptor antagonist — was FDA-approved in 2022 for EoE in patients ≥12 years. LIBERTY EoE TREET trial: histologic remission in ~60% vs. 5% placebo. It is the first biologic approved for EoE.

front: What are the fibrostenotic complications of untreated EoE?
back: Chronic eosinophilic inflammation drives subepithelial fibrosis, reducing esophageal distensibility. Consequences: esophageal strictures, fixed rings, food impaction ('steakhouse syndrome'), and esophageal perforation risk during food impaction dislodgement. Esophageal dilation treats strictures but does not address inflammation.

front: What is the 6-food elimination diet (6-FED) and which foods are removed?
back: The 6-FED removes: milk, wheat, egg, soy, nuts (tree nuts + peanuts), and seafood. Histologic remission rate ~72–80%. Foods are reintroduced sequentially with endoscopy after each reintroduction to identify culprit triggers. It is very restrictive with significant adherence burden.

front: What are the typical symptoms of EoE across age groups?
back: Infants/young children: feeding refusal, vomiting, failure to thrive. School-age children: vomiting, abdominal pain, GERD-like symptoms. Adolescents and adults: dysphagia (most common), food impaction. Older patients adapt by chewing extensively, eating slowly, and avoiding certain foods — masking symptom severity.

front: What are the NASPGHAN indications for H. pylori testing in children?
back: Testing is indicated for: peptic ulcer disease confirmed on endoscopy, first-degree relative with gastric cancer, and iron deficiency anemia refractory to iron supplementation. Testing is NOT recommended for functional dyspepsia alone, GERD, or as a routine screen. Test-and-treat without endoscopy is not recommended in children.

front: What is the recommended first-line H. pylori eradication regimen in children?
back: 14-day clarithromycin-based triple therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin) where clarithromycin resistance rates are <15%. If resistance is >15% or unknown: bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + amoxicillin). Duration matters: 14 days is superior to 7–10 days.

front: How is H. pylori eradication confirmed after treatment?
back: Urea breath test (UBT) is the preferred non-invasive confirmatory test — sensitivity/specificity >95%. Must be performed ≥4 weeks after antibiotics and ≥2 weeks after PPI discontinuation. Stool H. pylori antigen test is an acceptable alternative. Repeat endoscopy with culture is reserved for treatment failure.

front: What is the mechanism of H. pylori virulence via CagA?
back: CagA is injected into gastric epithelial cells via type IV secretion system, activating oncogenic signaling pathways (SRC, ERK). CagA-positive strains are associated with peptic ulcers and gastric cancer. VacA (vacuolating cytotoxin) disrupts mitochondrial function and induces apoptosis. CagA-positive strains carry highest cancer risk.

front: What is the relationship between H. pylori and iron deficiency anemia in children?
back: H. pylori gastritis impairs iron absorption (disrupting gastric acid needed for Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ reduction) and competes for iron. H. pylori eradication improves iron stores and hemoglobin in children with refractory iron deficiency anemia even without ulcer. NASPGHAN recommends H. pylori testing in IDA unresponsive to oral iron.

front: What effect do PPIs have on H. pylori diagnostic test accuracy?
back: PPIs suppress H. pylori density and urease activity. If taken within 2 weeks of testing: false-negative results on UBT, stool antigen test, rapid urease test (CLO), and histology. PPIs should be stopped ≥2 weeks before diagnostic testing. Serology is not affected by PPI use.

front: What is the management approach when first-line H. pylori therapy fails?
back: After first-line clarithromycin triple therapy failure: use bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + amoxicillin or tetracycline, 14 days). If second-line fails: endoscopy with culture and susceptibility testing to guide antibiotic selection (levofloxacin-based or rifabutin-based). Never repeat the same regimen twice.

front: What is the characteristic endoscopic finding of H. pylori gastritis in children?
back: Antral nodularity (lymphoid follicular hyperplasia — 'cobblestoning') is the most distinctive pediatric endoscopic finding of H. pylori gastritis. Erosions, erythema, and friability in the antrum are also common. Duodenal bulb ulcers strongly suggest H. pylori (or NSAIDs). Rapid urease test (CLO) on antral biopsies confirms active infection.

front: What is the relationship between H. pylori and gastric cancer risk?
back: H. pylori is classified as a Group 1 carcinogen (IARC). It is the primary cause of gastric adenocarcinoma and gastric MALT lymphoma. CagA-positive strains carry 3–5× higher gastric cancer risk. Gastric cancer risk is highest in East Asia, Eastern Europe, and Latin America. Latency from infection to cancer is typically decades.

front: What are the criteria for PPI duration after H. pylori eradication in peptic ulcer disease?
back: PPI therapy should continue for an additional 4–8 weeks after completing the antibiotic course for PUD (duodenal ulcers: 4 weeks; gastric ulcers: 8 weeks) to allow mucosal healing. For uncomplicated H. pylori gastritis without ulcer, PPI can be stopped after completing the eradication regimen.

front: What is gastroparesis and what are its diagnostic criteria?
back: Gastroparesis: delayed gastric emptying without mechanical obstruction. Requires symptoms (early satiety, nausea, vomiting, bloating) + objective evidence of delayed emptying on standardized 4-hour scintigraphy (>10% retention at 4 hours) in the absence of mechanical obstruction on endoscopy or imaging.

front: What are the most common causes of pediatric gastroparesis?
back: Idiopathic (most common in children, ~50%), post-viral (following viral gastroenteritis — often transient), post-surgical (fundoplication, cardiac surgery), autonomic neuropathy (diabetes mellitus, connective tissue disorders), and mitochondrial disease. Unlike adults (where diabetes is dominant), pediatric gastroparesis is predominantly idiopathic or post-viral.

front: What prokinetic agents are available for pediatric gastroparesis?
back: Erythromycin (motilin receptor agonist, 3 mg/kg/dose IV or oral TID): most potent prokinetic. Metoclopramide (D2 antagonist, also 5-HT4 agonist): FDA black box warning for tardive dyskinesia. Domperidone (peripheral D2 antagonist, fewer CNS effects): not FDA-approved in US but available abroad.

front: What is cyclical vomiting syndrome (CVS) and how does it differ from gastroparesis?
back: CVS: episodic, self-limited, stereotyped vomiting attacks lasting hours to days with symptom-free intervals, often triggered by stress or illness. Gastric emptying is typically normal between episodes. Gastroparesis has chronic continuous symptoms with objectively delayed emptying. CVS is treated with amitriptyline prophylaxis and abortive ondansetron.

front: What dietary modifications are recommended for pediatric gastroparesis?
back: Small, frequent meals (6 per day). Low-fat diet (<40 g/day) — fat delays gastric emptying. Low-fiber diet (insoluble fiber delays emptying). Liquid or pureed foods empty faster than solids. Avoid carbonated beverages. In severe cases: jejunal tube feeding bypasses gastric dysmotility.

front: What electrolyte abnormalities accompany severe vomiting from gastroparesis?
back: Hypokalemia (loss in vomit + alkalosis-driven cellular K+ shift), hypochloremia, metabolic alkalosis (loss of HCl), hyponatremia (if fluid intake is hypotonic), and hypomagnesemia with chronic vomiting. Dehydration causes elevated BUN/creatinine (prerenal azotemia). Treatment: IV NS + KCl supplementation.

front: What is the role of botulinum toxin injection into the pylorus for gastroparesis?
back: Endoscopic intrapyloric botulinum toxin injection (BTX-A, 100–200 units) reduces pyloric sphincter tone. However, two randomized controlled trials in adults showed no benefit over sham injection. Pediatric evidence is limited and mixed. BTX is not recommended as standard of care but is used in refractory cases at specialized centers.

front: What medications can cause or worsen gastroparesis?
back: Opioids (most common drug cause — μ-receptor activation inhibits GI motility). Anticholinergics (decrease antral contractility). GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) delay gastric emptying intentionally. Calcium channel blockers. These should be discontinued or minimized in gastroparesis patients.

front: What is gastric accommodation reflex and why is it relevant to gastroparesis?
back: Gastric accommodation is a vagally mediated reflex that allows the fundus to relax upon food ingestion, accommodating a meal without large pressure increases. Impaired accommodation causes early satiety and postprandial fullness — key symptoms of gastroparesis and functional dyspepsia. Buspirone and acotiamide enhance accommodation.

front: What is gastric electrical stimulation (GES) and in which gastroparesis patients is it used?
back: GES (Enterra Medical, FDA humanitarian device exemption) delivers high-frequency electrical stimulation via leads implanted in the gastric wall. It improves nausea/vomiting in drug-refractory idiopathic and diabetic gastroparesis by modulating afferent vagal signals (not by normalizing gastric emptying). Used in refractory cases, typically >12 years.

front: What is the Gross classification of esophageal atresia and what is the most common type?
back: Gross Type C (EA with distal TEF) is most common (~85%). Type A: isolated EA, no fistula (10%). Type B: EA with proximal TEF (<1%). Type E: H-type TEF without EA (4%) — presents with recurrent pneumonia and choking with feeds, no obvious feeding difficulty at birth.

front: What is the hallmark clinical presentation of pyloric stenosis?
back: Pyloric stenosis presents at 2–8 weeks with non-bilious, projectile, forceful vomiting after feeds. Ultrasound confirms: pyloric muscle thickness ≥4 mm, channel length ≥17 mm. Labs show hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. Treatment: fluid resuscitation, correct alkalosis, then Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.

front: What is malrotation and why is it a surgical emergency?
back: Malrotation is failure of normal 270-degree counterclockwise gut rotation, resulting in a narrow small bowel mesentery. The SMA can twist (midgut volvulus), causing complete mesenteric ischemia within 2–4 hours. Bilious vomiting in a neonate = malrotation until proven otherwise. Upper GI series confirms. Emergency Ladd's procedure required.

front: What is the absent rectoanal inhibitory reflex (RAIR) and how does it diagnose Hirschsprung disease?
back: The RAIR is normal relaxation of the internal anal sphincter upon rectal distension — mediated by intrinsic enteric neurons. In Hirschsprung disease, the aganglionic segment lacks these neurons, so RAIR is absent. Anorectal manometry demonstrating absent RAIR has >90% sensitivity for Hirschsprung disease.

front: What is Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) and what is its management?
back: HAEC is a life-threatening inflammatory complication of Hirschsprung disease. Features: fever, abdominal distension, explosive foul-smelling diarrhea, sepsis. X-ray: dilated colon proximal to aganglionic segment. Management: IV fluids, NPO, IV antibiotics (anaerobic + gram-negative coverage), rectal irrigations (30 mL saline qid) to decompress.

front: What is duodenal atresia and with which chromosomal condition is it associated?
back: Duodenal atresia is congenital obstruction of the duodenum — associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21) in ~30% of cases. X-ray shows the classic 'double bubble' sign (distended stomach + proximal duodenum, no distal gas). It presents with bilious vomiting at birth. Treatment: duodenoduodenostomy.

front: What is a Meckel's diverticulum and how is it diagnosed?
back: Meckel's diverticulum is the most common congenital GI anomaly (~2% of population) — remnant of the omphalomesenteric duct, within 2 feet of the ileocecal valve, typically 2 cm long, most symptomatic before age 2. Contains ectopic gastric or pancreatic mucosa. Painless lower GI bleeding is the classic presentation. Diagnosis: 99mTc pertechnetate (Meckel's) scan.

front: What is VACTERL association and which GI anomaly is its key component?
back: VACTERL: Vertebral, Anal atresia, Cardiac, Tracheo-Esophageal fistula, Renal, Limb anomalies. EA/TEF is the GI component — occurring in ~30% of VACTERL cases. Any infant with EA/TEF should be screened for other VACTERL components (echocardiogram, renal ultrasound, spine X-ray).

front: What is meconium ileus and what proportion of cases are associated with cystic fibrosis?
back: Meconium ileus is obstruction of the terminal ileum by thick inspissated meconium — associated with CF in >90% of cases. ~15–20% of all CF patients present with meconium ileus at birth. Simple meconium ileus: gastrografin enema (hyperosmolar — draws water into bowel). Complicated: surgical repair.

front: What is the most common cause of lower GI bleeding in infants under 6 months?
back: Milk protein (cow's milk or soy) proctocolitis is the most common cause of rectal bleeding in infants under 6 months — bright red blood in otherwise well-appearing infants. Management: maternal dairy elimination (if breastfed) or switch to extensively hydrolyzed formula. Almost always resolves by 12 months.

front: What is intestinal atresia and how does it differ from malrotation?
back: Intestinal atresia is congenital complete obstruction due to intrauterine vascular injury — no bowel rotation problem. Duodenal atresia: associated with Down syndrome, double bubble X-ray. Jejunal/ileal atresia: 'apple peel' (type IIIb) in CF; colonic atresia is rare. All present with bilious vomiting and abdominal distension. Malrotation involves abnormal bowel rotation with volvulus risk.

front: What is the omphalocele versus gastroschisis distinction?
back: Omphalocele: defect at umbilicus with intestinal contents covered by a peritoneal sac; associated with other anomalies (cardiac, chromosomal — Beckwith-Wiedemann, trisomies). Gastroschisis: lateral abdominal wall defect (usually right of umbilicus), no sac, bowel exposed to amniotic fluid; usually isolated without chromosomal abnormalities.

front: What HLA haplotypes are required for celiac disease susceptibility?
back: HLA-DQ2 (DQA1*05/DQB1*02): present in ~90% of celiac patients. HLA-DQ8 (DQA1*03/DQB1*0302): present in ~5%. ~5% carry neither — celiac is essentially excluded (strong negative predictive value). However, >30% of the general population carries DQ2 or DQ8 without developing celiac.

front: What is the Marsh-Oberhuber classification of celiac histopathology?
back: Marsh 0: normal. Marsh 1: increased IELs (>25/100 enterocytes), normal villi. Marsh 2: crypt hyperplasia + increased IELs. Marsh 3a: partial villous atrophy. Marsh 3b: subtotal villous atrophy. Marsh 3c: total villous atrophy (flat mucosa). Marsh 3 lesions confirm celiac when combined with positive serology.

front: What is refractory celiac disease type II and its malignant potential?
back: RCD Type II: clonal aberrant IELs (CD3+, CD8−, intracytoplasmic CD3+) — 50–80% risk of developing enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL), a highly aggressive lymphoma. RCD Type I has polyclonal IELs, better prognosis, responds to steroids/azathioprine. Diagnosis: EATL on small bowel imaging/biopsy.

front: What conditions require celiac disease screening in children?
back: First-degree relatives of celiac patients, type 1 diabetes mellitus, Down syndrome (5–12% prevalence), Turner syndrome (~4%), Williams syndrome, selective IgA deficiency, and autoimmune thyroid disease. Screen with anti-tTG IgA + total IgA. Screen children with T1DM at diagnosis and every 2 years for 5 years.

front: What is the significance of IgA deficiency in celiac disease serology?
back: Selective IgA deficiency (serum IgA <7 mg/dL) affects ~1 in 50 celiac patients — 10× higher than general population. IgA-based serology (anti-tTG IgA, anti-EMA IgA) will be falsely negative in IgA-deficient patients. Always check total IgA simultaneously. If IgA deficient: use anti-tTG IgG or anti-DGP IgG.

front: What dietary grains are safe and unsafe in celiac disease?
back: Unsafe: wheat (including spelt, kamut, farro, triticale), rye, barley. Oats: naturally gluten-free but frequently contaminated — only certified gluten-free oats in controlled amounts are safe; some celiac patients react to avenin. Safe grains: rice, corn, quinoa, amaranth, buckwheat, millet, sorghum, teff.

front: What is the gluten challenge and how much gluten must be consumed before reliable testing?
back: For patients already on a GFD: reintroduce gluten ≥3 g/day (approximately 2 slices of bread) for ≥6–8 weeks before serology and ≥8–12 weeks before endoscopy. Shorter challenges produce false negatives. This is important for children who started GFD before biopsy confirmation.

front: What monitoring is recommended for children with celiac disease after starting a gluten-free diet?
back: Anti-tTG IgA (and total IgA): at 6 and 12 months after GFD initiation, then annually. CBC, iron studies, vitamin D, ferritin: baseline and annually for 1–2 years. Dietitian follow-up for GFD adherence and nutritional adequacy (GFD is often low in fiber, B vitamins, and iron).

front: What is dermatitis herpetiformis and how is it related to celiac disease?
back: DH is an intensely pruritic, vesicular rash on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp) — IgA-mediated cutaneous manifestation of celiac disease. Skin biopsy shows granular IgA deposits in dermal papillae (pathognomonic). GFD resolves DH over months; dapsone provides rapid symptom control. All patients with DH have celiac disease.

front: What is non-celiac gluten sensitivity (NCGS)?
back: NCGS is a clinical syndrome of GI and extra-intestinal symptoms triggered by gluten in patients without celiac disease (negative serology, normal histology) and without wheat allergy. Pathophysiology involves innate immunity. Diagnosis is by exclusion + symptom improvement on GFD + recurrence on gluten challenge. No biomarker exists.

front: How does the stool osmotic gap distinguish osmotic from secretory diarrhea?
back: Stool osmotic gap = 290 − 2 × (stool Na + stool K). Osmotic diarrhea: gap >125 mOsm/kg (poorly absorbed solutes retain water; resolves with fasting). Secretory diarrhea: gap <50 mOsm/kg (active ion secretion; persists with fasting). Major osmotic causes: malabsorption, osmotic laxatives. Secretory causes: cholera, VIP-oma, bile acid malabsorption.

front: What is the fecal elastase threshold for diagnosing severe exocrine pancreatic insufficiency?
back: Fecal elastase-1 (FE-1) <100 mcg/g = severe EPI (requires PERT). 100–200 mcg/g = moderate EPI. >200 mcg/g = normal. FE-1 is stable in stool (not degraded by transit) and is the preferred non-invasive test for EPI. Watery diarrhea can falsely lower values — repeat on formed stool.

front: What is the mechanism and clinical features of bile acid malabsorption?
back: Secondary (most common): ileal resection or Crohn's ileitis reduces bile acid reabsorption → bile acids enter colon → secretory diarrhea. Primary: genetic defect in ileal ASBT transporter (rare). Treatment: cholestyramine (binds bile acids in colon). Ileal Crohn's disease resection >100 cm: hepatic bile acid synthesis can't compensate.

front: What is protein-losing enteropathy (PLE) and how is it diagnosed?
back: PLE is excessive GI protein loss causing hypoalbuminemia disproportionate to hepatic synthetic dysfunction. Causes: lymphangiectasia, IBD, Ménétrier disease, cardiac (Fontan). Diagnosis: 24-hour stool alpha-1-AT clearance >24 mL/day. Low albumin + normal liver function (no elevated PT/INR) should prompt PLE evaluation.

front: What is intestinal lymphangiectasia and how does it cause malabsorption?
back: Primary intestinal lymphangiectasia: dilated lacteal lymphatics in small bowel lamina propria → lymph leaks into lumen → loss of fat, fat-soluble vitamins, proteins, and lymphocytes. Endoscopy: white speckling of villi. Treatment: very low-fat diet + medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs — absorbed directly into portal blood, bypass lacteals).

front: What is toddler's diarrhea and how is it managed?
back: Toddler's diarrhea (Rome IV: 'functional diarrhea') affects children ages 1–4 years — 3–10 loose stools/day with undigested food, normal growth, no blood, no nocturnal symptoms. Caused by excessive fruit juice/fructose intake and rapid intestinal transit. Management: limit juice to <4 oz/day, reduce sorbitol foods, normalize fat intake. Resolves by ~4 years.

front: What is the 72-hour fecal fat test threshold for steatorrhea?
back: On a standard 100 g/day fat diet, stool fat >7 g/day (>7% of intake) in children >6 months indicates pathologic steatorrhea. Steatorrhea indicates either pancreatic exocrine insufficiency or small bowel mucosal malabsorption. The Sudan III spot test (qualitative) is only ~70% sensitive.

front: What is abetalipoproteinemia and why does it cause fat-soluble vitamin deficiency?
back: Abetalipoproteinemia: autosomal recessive MTP mutation — prevents chylomicron and VLDL formation in enterocytes. Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) cannot be transported from the intestine (depend on chylomicrons). Features: severe steatorrhea from birth, acanthocytosis, spinocerebellar ataxia, retinitis pigmentosa. Treatment: very low-fat diet + high-dose fat-soluble vitamins.

front: What is the significance of elevated IELs on duodenal biopsy without villous atrophy?
back: Elevated IELs (>25/100 enterocytes, Marsh 1) without villous atrophy has a broad differential: celiac disease (most common), H. pylori gastritis (duodenal IELs), NSAID enteropathy, tropical sprue, giardiasis, Crohn's disease, CVID, IPEX syndrome. Must correlate with celiac serology and clinical context before diagnosing celiac.

front: What are the clinical features of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency (CSID)?
back: CSID: autosomal recessive defect in sucrase-isomaltase — inability to digest sucrose and starch. Presents with osmotic diarrhea, bloating, and pain upon introduction of sucrose-containing foods in infancy. Hydrogen breath test after sucrose load is positive. Treatment: sacrosidase enzyme replacement (Sucraid), sucrose-restricted diet.

front: What are the causes of vitamin B12 malabsorption in children?
back: Dietary deficiency (vegan diet — most common in infants of vegan mothers), ileal disease or resection (Crohn's ileitis, short bowel — B12 absorbed at terminal ileum via IF receptor), SIBO (bacteria consume B12), pernicious anemia (anti-IF antibodies — rare in children), and Immerslund-Gräsbeck syndrome (defect in ileal B12 receptor).

front: What is the normal villous-to-crypt ratio in the duodenum and what ratio indicates pathologic atrophy?
back: Normal villous-to-crypt ratio: 3:1 to 5:1 (tall finger-like villi, small crypts). In celiac disease (Marsh 3): ratio approaches 1:1 (flattened villi, elongated crypts — crypt hyperplasia). Severe atrophy: completely flat mucosa. Increased IELs >25/100 enterocytes is the most sensitive histologic marker of gluten injury.

front: What is Whipple disease and when is it suspected in children?
back: Whipple disease: chronic systemic infection with Tropheryma whipplei. Features: malabsorption, weight loss, arthralgias (often years before GI symptoms), fever, lymphadenopathy, CNS disease. Extremely rare in children. PAS-positive macrophages in duodenal biopsy are pathognomonic. PCR for T. whipplei confirms. Treatment: TMP-SMX for 1–2 years.

front: What is the diagnostic criterion for small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)?
back: Gold standard: duodenal/jejunal aspirate culture >10⁵ CFU/mL. Non-invasive: glucose or lactulose hydrogen breath test — a rise of >20 ppm above baseline within 90 minutes of glucose ingestion suggests SIBO. Glucose breath test has ~77% sensitivity, ~46% specificity.

front: What antibiotic is preferred for SIBO treatment in children?
back: Rifaximin is the preferred antibiotic for SIBO — non-absorbable, broad-spectrum, minimal systemic effects. Pediatric dosing: ~10 mg/kg/dose TID × 14 days (weight-based). Alternative: metronidazole, amoxicillin-clavulanate. Underlying cause (dysmotility, short bowel, anatomic) must be addressed to prevent recurrence.

front: What are the indications for fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) in children?
back: FMT is FDA-approved for recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection — at least 2 recurrences, or 1 recurrence with severe/fulminant disease. Pediatric FMT efficacy for recurrent CDI: ~85–90%. FMT for IBD and other conditions remains investigational. Should not be used outside recurrent CDI without IRB approval.

front: What probiotic has the strongest evidence for preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea in children?
back: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LGG) has the strongest evidence — meta-analyses show ~50% risk reduction for antibiotic-associated diarrhea. Saccharomyces boulardii also has significant evidence. Dose: 10 billion CFU/day during and for 2 weeks after antibiotic course. Avoid in severely immunocompromised patients (septicemia risk).

front: What is butyrate and why is it important for colonic health?
back: Butyrate is a short-chain fatty acid produced by anaerobic fermentation of dietary fiber by colonic bacteria (Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Roseburia). It is the primary energy source for colonocytes (~70% of colonocyte energy needs), maintains epithelial barrier integrity, has anti-inflammatory effects, and promotes Treg differentiation. Reduced butyrate is a feature of IBD-associated dysbiosis.

front: What is the treatment for Clostridioides difficile infection in children?
back: Non-severe CDI: vancomycin oral 125 mg QID × 10 days (preferred over metronidazole per IDSA 2021). Fidaxomicin is also preferred but expensive. Severe CDI (WBC >15,000 or creatinine elevated): oral vancomycin 500 mg QID ± IV metronidazole. Fulminant (ileus, megacolon, hypotension): surgical colectomy may be required. FMT for ≥3rd recurrence.

front: What C. difficile testing algorithm is recommended in children?
back: Two-step testing: NAAT (PCR) for C. diff toxin gene — high sensitivity but detects colonization too. Toxin EIA: high specificity, lower sensitivity. Algorithm: NAAT positive + toxin EIA positive = active CDI requiring treatment. Children <2 years are frequently colonized — positive C. diff in this age group does not necessarily require treatment.

front: What is methane-predominant SIBO and what is its clinical significance?
back: Methane is produced by methanogenic archaea fermenting hydrogen in the colon/small bowel. Methane-predominant SIBO (breath methane >10 ppm) is associated with constipation-predominant IBS — methane slows intestinal transit. Rifaximin alone is less effective; adding neomycin improves eradication. Requires combined H2+CH4 breath testing.

front: What is the role of breast milk in shaping the infant gut microbiome?
back: Breast milk contains: live bacteria (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium), human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs — selectively feed Bifidobacterium longum), secretory IgA, lactoferrin, and lysozyme. Breastfed infants have Bifidobacterium-dominant microbiomes (protective against allergy, NEC, CDI). Formula-fed infants have more diverse but less protective flora.

front: What is the relationship between early antibiotic exposure and IBD risk?
back: Multiple epidemiologic studies show antibiotic exposure in the first year of life is associated with increased IBD risk (Crohn's particularly) — OR ~1.5–3×. Mechanism: antibiotic-induced dysbiosis during a critical window of immune programming. Association strongest with broad-spectrum antibiotics. Supports prudent antibiotic prescribing in infancy.

front: What is the difference between a prebiotic and a probiotic?
back: Probiotic: live microorganisms that confer health benefits when administered in adequate amounts (e.g., LGG, S. boulardii). Prebiotic: non-digestible dietary fibers selectively metabolized by beneficial gut bacteria, promoting their growth (e.g., HMOs, inulin, FOS). Synbiotic: combination of both. Postbiotic: bioactive compounds produced by probiotic bacteria (e.g., butyrate).

front: What role does secretory IgA (sIgA) play in gut immunity?
back: sIgA is the predominant immunoglobulin in intestinal secretions. It coats bacteria (immune exclusion), limits bacterial adherence to epithelium, and modulates microbiome composition. IgA deficiency predisposes to bacterial overgrowth and GI infections. sIgA is produced by lamina propria plasma cells and transcytosed by the poly-Ig receptor.

front: What bowel length is associated with permanent intestinal failure requiring long-term PN?
back: Remaining small bowel <30–40 cm without an ileocecal valve, or <50–75 cm with ileocecal valve, is associated with high risk of permanent intestinal failure. The ileocecal valve significantly improves prognosis by slowing transit and reducing bacterial overgrowth.

front: What is intestinal adaptation and how long does it take after bowel resection?
back: Intestinal adaptation: residual bowel undergoes structural changes (villous hyperplasia, crypt deepening) to increase absorptive capacity. Adaptation continues for 1–2 years in children. Stimulated by luminal nutrients (EGF, GLP-2). Early introduction of enteral feeds is critical — TPN alone is insufficient to promote adaptation.

front: What is teduglutide and what is its mechanism in pediatric short bowel syndrome?
back: Teduglutide (Gattex/Revestive): GLP-2 analog that stimulates intestinal epithelial growth and reduces gastric acid secretion and intestinal transit. FDA-approved for SBS in children ≥1 year. Dose: 0.05 mg/kg/day SC. Studies show increased villous height and reduced PN volume requirements. Contraindicated if active GI malignancy.

front: What is intestinal failure-associated liver disease (IFALD)?
back: IFALD: progressive cholestatic liver disease (conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, elevated GGT, fibrosis) in patients on long-term PN. Causes: hepatotoxic plant sterols in soy-based lipids, excess carbohydrates, sepsis, lack of enteral stimulation, SIBO. Prevention: fish-oil lipid (SMOFlipid), cycle PN, maximize enteral feeds, treat SIBO.

front: What is the STEP procedure in short bowel syndrome?
back: Serial Transverse Enteroplasty (STEP): a linear stapler serially divides a dilated segment of bowel transversely, creating a longer, narrower bowel that functions more effectively. It increases functional surface area, reduces bacterial stasis, and improves motility. Performed when remaining bowel is dilated and dysmotile despite optimal medical management.

front: What is D-lactic acidosis and in which short bowel syndrome patients does it occur?
back: D-lactic acidosis: colonic bacteria ferment unabsorbed carbohydrates to D-lactate, absorbed into the bloodstream. Humans lack D-lactate dehydrogenase — causes metabolic acidosis with neurologic symptoms (ataxia, confusion, slurred speech). Occurs in SBS patients with intact colon receiving high-carbohydrate diets. Standard lactate assays measure only L-lactate — must order D-lactate specifically.

front: What is the role of the ileocecal valve in short bowel syndrome prognosis?
back: The ileocecal valve (ICV) slows intestinal transit, prevents colonic bacterial retrograde migration into the small bowel (reduces SIBO), and marks the functional boundary between small and large bowel. Presence of the ICV dramatically improves prognosis — children with ≥30 cm + intact ICV are likely to achieve enteral autonomy.

front: What monitoring priorities apply in a child with short bowel syndrome?
back: Weight, height, growth velocity (monthly); serum vitamins A, D, E, K, B12, folate; zinc, magnesium, selenium, essential fatty acids; stool output volume (>30 mL/kg/day = excessive losses); ostomy output electrolytes. Vitamin B12 injections monthly if terminal ileum resected. Ileostomy patients lose 5–15× more Zn per liter of output.

front: What complications are associated with central venous catheter dependence in intestinal failure?
back: Central line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSIs) are the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in intestinal failure — each infection risks IFALD progression, catheter loss, and septic complications. Line care protocols (alcohol caps, aseptic technique, ethanol locks) reduce CLABSI rates. Other complications: catheter thrombosis, cardiac complications, psychological burden.

front: What is intestinal transplantation and when is it indicated in pediatric SBS?
back: Intestinal transplantation is reserved for: (1) life-threatening PN-related complications (liver failure/IFALD, recurrent CLABSIs, thrombosis of major central veins), or (2) ultra-short bowel with no hope of adaptation. 5-year survival ~50–60%. Significant morbidity from graft-versus-host disease, infection, and rejection.

front: What is the normal length of the small bowel at term birth and how does it change with age?
back: At term: 200–250 cm. By adulthood: 300–800 cm (average ~600 cm). This 3–4× increase occurs during the first year of life, driven by growth and enteral nutrition. A neonate with 30 cm of bowel may achieve 100–120 cm by age 2 with careful management — potentially sufficient for enteral autonomy.

front: What is exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) in Crohn's disease and what is its remission rate?
back: EEN provides 100% of nutritional requirements as formula for 6–8 weeks, eliminating regular food. Remission rate in pediatric Crohn's disease: ~80–85% (superior to prednisolone for mucosal healing in some RCTs). It avoids steroid side effects and improves nutritional status. Recommended as first-line induction in children by ECCO/ESPGHAN.

front: What is the infliximab dosing regimen for induction in pediatric Crohn's disease?
back: Infliximab (IFX) induction: 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, and 6. Maintenance: 5 mg/kg every 8 weeks. Dose escalation (10 mg/kg or shortened interval) for inadequate response or subtherapeutic trough levels. Target trough: >5 mcg/mL. Proactive therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is associated with improved outcomes.

front: What is the Paris modification of the Montreal classification for pediatric Crohn's disease?
back: Paris classification: L1 distal ileum ± limited cecal disease, L2 colonic, L3 ileocolonic, L4a/b upper GI. B1 non-stricturing non-penetrating, B2 stricturing, B3 penetrating. G0 no growth failure, G1 growth failure. Perianal disease (P) noted separately. This stratification guides treatment intensity.

front: What is the treat-to-target strategy in pediatric Crohn's disease?
back: Treat-to-target (T2T) aims for mucosal healing (not just symptom control) as the treatment endpoint. Target: endoscopic remission (SES-CD <3) + symptom remission + normalized CRP and FC. Assess response at 3–6 months with labs/FC/MRE; adjust therapy if targets not met. T2T reduces hospitalizations and surgeries.

front: What is TPMT and why is it checked before starting azathioprine?
back: Azathioprine (AZA) is converted to active 6-thioguanine (6-TGN) metabolites by TPMT. TPMT deficiency leads to 6-TGN accumulation → severe myelosuppression. Check TPMT genotype or phenotype before starting AZA/6-MP. If TPMT deficient: avoid thiopurines. NUDT15 polymorphism (East Asian patients) also affects toxicity risk.

front: What perianal disease features in Crohn's disease should prompt anti-TNF therapy?
back: Complex perianal fistulas (transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, multiple tracts), abscesses, and symptomatic fistulas are indications for anti-TNF therapy (infliximab preferred). Surgical drainage of abscesses before starting biologics. Setons may be placed to control fistula while biologic therapy is initiated.

front: What is ustekinumab and when is it used in pediatric Crohn's disease?
back: Ustekinumab is an anti-IL-12/23 monoclonal antibody (targets p40 subunit). FDA-approved for adult CD in 2016; pediatric off-label use is increasing. Indication: moderate-severe CD failing anti-TNF therapy. Induction: single IV weight-based dose; maintenance: 90 mg SC every 8–12 weeks. Favorable safety profile.

front: What are the risk factors for a disabling Crohn's disease course in children?
back: Risk factors: young age at diagnosis (<6 years), deep mucosal ulcers on initial endoscopy, penetrating behavior, perianal fistulizing disease, upper GI involvement (L4), growth failure at diagnosis, severe hypoalbuminemia, steroids required at first flare, and extensive disease on MRE. These 'high-risk' features prompt early combination therapy.

front: What is the role of methotrexate as maintenance therapy in pediatric Crohn's disease?
back: Methotrexate (MTX) is an immunomodulator for CD patients intolerant to thiopurines (AZA/6-MP). Dose: 15 mg/m² SC or IM weekly. Reduces anti-drug antibody formation when combined with infliximab. Monitor: CBC, LFTs monthly. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Folate supplementation (1 mg/day) reduces toxicity.

front: What is the PCDAI (Pediatric Crohn's Disease Activity Index) and what score indicates remission?
back: PCDAI scores abdominal pain, stool frequency, well-being, growth, physical exam, and lab values (hematocrit, ESR, albumin). Remission: PCDAI ≤10. Mild disease: 11–30. Moderate: 31–51. Severe: >51. A score decrease of ≥12.5 points is a clinically meaningful response.

front: What surgical indications prompt bowel resection in pediatric Crohn's disease?
back: Fibrostenotic stricture causing obstructive symptoms unresponsive to medical therapy, intra-abdominal abscess not responding to antibiotics/biologic, medically refractory disease with failure of multiple biologics, isolated ileocecal disease with growth failure (ileocecal resection may be superior to continued medical therapy — CRIUR trial), perforation, and malignancy.

front: What is the colorectal cancer risk in pediatric-onset Crohn's colitis?
back: Pediatric-onset Crohn's colitis with extensive colonic involvement carries CRC risk of ~8% at 20 years and ~18% at 30 years from diagnosis — similar to UC with equivalent extent. Colonoscopic surveillance starts 8–10 years after diagnosis, every 1–3 years depending on risk.

front: What is the PUCAI score and what defines remission?
back: PUCAI scores 6 clinical features (abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, stool consistency, nocturnal stools, activity level, stool frequency). Total score 0–85. Remission: PUCAI <10. Mild: 10–34. Moderate: 35–64. Severe: ≥65. PUCAI Day 3 score ≥45 during IV steroids predicts steroid failure and triggers rescue therapy.

front: What rescue therapy options exist for acute severe pediatric UC failing IV corticosteroids?
back: After 3–5 days of IV corticosteroids with PUCAI Day 3 ≥45: (1) Infliximab (IFX) — 5 mg/kg IV (most commonly used); (2) Cyclosporine 2–4 mg/kg/day continuous IV (trough 150–250 ng/mL, nephrotoxicity risk); (3) Tacrolimus (oral). IFX is preferred in most centers. Surgical colectomy if medical rescue fails.

front: What histologic features distinguish ulcerative colitis from Crohn's colitis?
back: UC: mucosal-only inflammation, continuous from rectum, cryptitis, crypt abscesses, goblet cell depletion, basal plasmacytosis. Crohn's colitis: transmural inflammation, skip lesions, non-caseating granulomas (~50%), perianal disease, upper GI involvement. ~10% of children have IBD-U (indeterminate colitis) — cannot be classified on histology.

front: What is the role of 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) in pediatric UC?
back: 5-ASA (mesalamine, sulfasalazine) is first-line maintenance for mild-moderate UC. Mechanism: local anti-inflammatory via NF-κB inhibition. Dose: mesalamine 40–60 mg/kg/day (max 4.8 g/day). Combined oral + rectal dosing is superior to either alone for distal disease. Annual urine dipstick monitoring for interstitial nephritis (rare but serious side effect).

front: What is vedolizumab and what is its mechanism in IBD?
back: Vedolizumab is an anti-α4β7 integrin antibody that blocks lymphocyte trafficking specifically to gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT). Gut-selective mechanism — does not affect systemic immunity. Approved for adult IBD; pediatric off-label use growing. Useful in patients with systemic anti-TNF concerns (infections, demyelination risk).

front: What dysplasia surveillance protocol applies to pediatric UC?
back: Colonoscopic surveillance starts 8–10 years after UC diagnosis for extensive or left-sided colitis. Frequency: every 1–2 years for high-risk (PSC, stricture, prior dysplasia, family history of CRC before age 50) or every 2–3 years for standard risk. Technique: chromoendoscopy (dye spray) with targeted biopsies.

front: What is primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) and its association with pediatric UC?
back: PSC is a chronic cholestatic disease with fibro-inflammatory stricturing of bile ducts. ~70% of pediatric PSC cases are associated with IBD (predominantly UC — pancolitis with relative rectal sparing). Diagnosis: MRCP shows biliary beading/stricturing. No proven medical therapy delays progression. PSC-IBD warrants annual colonoscopic CRC surveillance.

front: What are the extraintestinal manifestations of IBD in children?
back: EIMs affect ~25–40% of pediatric IBD patients. Parallel disease activity: peripheral arthropathy, erythema nodosum, aphthous ulcers, episcleritis. Independent of bowel activity: pyoderma gangrenosum, uveitis (may be asymptomatic — ophthalmologic screening recommended), ankylosing spondylitis (HLA-B27 associated), PSC.

front: What is tofacitinib and when is it used in pediatric UC?
back: Tofacitinib is a JAK1/3 inhibitor. FDA-approved for adult UC; used off-label in refractory pediatric UC. Mechanism: blocks JAK-STAT signaling, rapidly reducing mucosal cytokines. Dose: 10 mg BID induction × 8 weeks, then 5 mg BID maintenance. Safety concerns: herpes zoster risk, thromboembolic risk (black box warning >5 mg BID). Requires varicella immunity before starting.

front: What is pouchitis and how is it managed?
back: Pouchitis is inflammation of the ileal J-pouch after IPAA — occurring in 50% of UC patients within 5 years. Symptoms: increased stool frequency, urgency, pelvic pain, bleeding. Treatment: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 14 days (first-line) or metronidazole. Chronic pouchitis: antibiotics alternating, probiotic VSL#3, budesonide enemas, or biologics (vedolizumab, anti-TNF) for refractory cases.

front: What prophylaxis is required before initiating anti-TNF therapy in a child with UC?
back: Before anti-TNF initiation: TST or IGRA for latent TB (treat with isoniazid if positive), hepatitis B serology (reactivation risk), review vaccination status (live vaccines must be given ≥3–4 weeks before starting biologic — contraindicated on anti-TNF), varicella/MMR vaccination if not immune. Screen for strongyloides in endemic regions.

front: What is cytomegalovirus (CMV) colitis and its significance in acute severe UC?
back: CMV reactivation occurs in ~30% of steroid-refractory UC patients — worsening inflammation and causing apparent steroid resistance. Detection: CMV colitis (>5 CMV inclusion bodies/10 HPF on biopsy, or CMV DNA >250 copies/mg tissue). Treatment: ganciclovir IV or oral valganciclovir for 2–3 weeks. Always evaluate for CMV before colectomy.

front: What is the definition and management of very early onset IBD (VEO-IBD)?
back: VEO-IBD: IBD diagnosed before age 6 years. Distinct phenotype: predominance of colitis (UC-like or IBD-U), high rates of monogenic IBD (IL-10 pathway defects, XIAP deficiency, LRBA, CGD), poor response to conventional therapies. VEO-IBD should prompt whole exome or gene panel sequencing — monogenic causes present in 15–30% of children diagnosed before age 5.

front: What are the Rome IV criteria for functional constipation in children ages 4–18 years?
back: Rome IV requires ≥2 of the following at least once per week for ≥1 month: ≤2 spontaneous defecations/week, history of excessive stool retention, painful or hard bowel movements, large-diameter stools that may obstruct the toilet, presence of a large fecal mass in the rectum. No structural or metabolic explanation.

front: What is the PEG 3350 dose for fecal disimpaction versus maintenance?
back: Disimpaction: PEG 3350 1.5 g/kg/day (max 100 g/day) for 3–6 days — 1–3 watery stools per day indicate success. Maintenance: 0.4–0.8 g/kg/day (target one soft stool per day). Maintenance should continue for 2+ months and be tapered slowly after regular toileting is established.

front: What alarm features in a child with constipation warrant investigation for organic cause?
back: Alarm features: onset in first month of life, meconium passage >48 hours after birth, family history of Hirschsprung disease, ribbon stools, blood in stool without anal fissure, failure to thrive, fever, severe abdominal distension, sacral dimple or hair patch, absent anal wink, decreased lower extremity reflexes. Suggest Hirschsprung, spinal cord lesion, hypothyroidism, CF.

front: What is encopresis and how does it relate to functional constipation?
back: Encopresis (fecal soiling) in a toilet-trained child is almost always overflow incontinence from functional constipation — liquid stool leaks around a large fecal mass. Families often perceive it as behavioral defiance. Treatment: disimpaction + maintenance PEG + scheduled toilet sitting + positive reinforcement. Encopresis resolves when constipation is successfully treated.

front: What is biofeedback therapy and when is it used in pediatric constipation?
back: Biofeedback uses visual or auditory feedback during anorectal manometry exercises to teach a child to relax the external anal sphincter during defecation — treating dyssynergic defecation (paradoxical puborectalis contraction). Effective for children ≥4 years with confirmed dyssynergia on manometry. Does not help slow-transit constipation.

front: What medications are used for refractory pediatric functional constipation beyond PEG?
back: Stimulant laxatives (senna, bisacodyl) for short-term use. Lubiprostone (ClC-2 channel activator): approved for FC ages 6–17 years (2020). Linaclotide (GC-C agonist): approved for FC ≥6 years. Prucalopride (5-HT4 agonist): licensed in EU for children. Enemas (sodium phosphate, saline): reserved for acute disimpaction or refractory cases.

front: What lifestyle recommendations supplement laxative therapy for pediatric constipation?
back: Dietary fiber: age + 5 grams/day. Fluid intake: 1–1.5 L/day of water. Regular toilet sitting: 5–10 minutes after each meal (exploit gastrocolic reflex), feet on footstool. Limit cow's milk to ≤16 oz/day. Physical activity. Positive reinforcement for sitting (not stool output). Maintain for months.

front: How is the anorectal inhibitory reflex (RAIR) used to diagnose Hirschsprung disease?
back: RAIR: internal anal sphincter relaxation upon rectal distension. In Hirschsprung disease, RAIR is absent (aganglionic segment lacks enteric neurons). Absent RAIR has >90% sensitivity for Hirschsprung. False negatives occur if balloon is too small. Rectal suction biopsy (absent ganglion cells + increased AChE staining) remains gold standard.

front: What is slow-transit constipation and how is it diagnosed?
back: Slow-transit constipation: reduced colonic motor activity causing prolonged stool transit. Diagnosis: colonic transit study with Sitzmarks radio-opaque markers — 5 days after swallowing 24 markers, >5 remaining in any colonic region indicates delayed transit. Alternatively, wireless motility capsule (SmartPill). Prucalopride or surgical colectomy in extreme refractory cases.

front: What is the recommended duration of maintenance laxative therapy for functional constipation?
back: Maintenance laxative therapy (PEG) should continue for minimum 2 months after achieving regular, comfortable defecation. Many children require 6–24 months. Premature discontinuation is the most common reason for relapse. Taper dose gradually (by 25% every 4 weeks). Up to 20–30% of children with FC continue to have symptoms into adulthood.

front: What is anismus (dyssynergic defecation)?
back: Anismus: paradoxical contraction of the puborectalis muscle or external anal sphincter during defecation, causing functional outlet obstruction despite intact colonic transit.
